originalTitle: Women secretly filmed, then ridiculed and abused online
title: Stay Safe, Be Kind: Important News from Africa!
content:
Hello, amazing young friends from all over our beautiful continent of Africa! This is your friendly African news reporter, ready to share some very important news with you today. We're going to talk about something super important: being kind, being safe, and respecting each other, especially when we are online. You know, like when you watch videos or play games on a tablet or a computer? It's like a big adventure, but we need to know the rules to stay happy and safe! This story comes from two lovely African countries, and it teaches us a very big lesson about kindness and safety. Let's learn together!

What Happened?

Sometimes, grown-ups called ladies and women were in two lovely countries in Africa, called Kenya and Ghana. These countries are full of sunshine and friendly smiles, just like many places you know! A man from a far-off land called Russia came to visit. But something not very kind happened. This man took videos of these ladies without asking them first. He didn't say, 'May I please film you?' And they didn't say 'yes.' He just filmed them secretly. He did it without them knowing he was doing it. Did you know? Kenya is famous for its amazing wildlife, like lions and giraffes! And Ghana is known for its delicious jollof rice and beautiful kente cloth! These are both wonderful places in our big African family. After filming them secretly, this man then put these videos on the internet for everyone to see. When the videos were online, some people who watched them started to say mean things about the ladies. They wrote unkind words and made fun of them. This made the ladies feel very, very sad and upset. Imagine someone playing with your favourite toy without asking you. How would that make you feel? Maybe a little bit sad, right? Or maybe a little bit angry because it's your toy. Well, taking someone's picture or video without asking is a bit like that. It's not nice, and it's not respectful of their space.

What is Permission?

This brings us to a big, important word: Permission! Can you say 'permission'? It means asking someone if it's okay to do something, and they say 'yes.' If they say 'no,' then we don't do it. It's super simple! Think about it: * Do you ask your friend before you borrow their crayon? Yes! * Do you ask your grown-up if you can have a cookie? Yes! * Do you ask before you hug someone? Yes! Permission is all about respect. It means we care about what other people want and how they feel. Everyone has the right to say 'yes' or 'no' when someone wants to take their picture or video. It's their body, their face, their choice! Nobody should ever take a picture or video of you, or anyone else, without asking first. And if they do ask, you always have the right to say 'no.' Did you know? Asking for permission makes everyone feel happier and safer! It shows you are a thoughtful and kind person who respects others.

What is 'Online'?

Now, let's talk about 'online.' What does 'online' mean? When you use a computer, a tablet, or a phone to connect to the internet, you are 'online.' It's like a giant, super-fast message system that connects almost everyone in the world! You can watch your favourite cartoons, learn new things, or even talk to your grandma who lives far away. The internet is like a very, very big library, a very big playground, and a very big post office all rolled into one! But just like any big playground, we need to be careful when we are online. Some parts of the internet are super fun and safe, but some parts are not. It's like a real road – you wouldn't cross the road without holding a grown-up's hand, right? Being online is similar. We need to be careful about what we see and what we share. It's important to remember that once something is online, it's very hard to take it back. Many people can see it, even if you don't know them. Did you know? The internet can help people in Africa learn about new things, connect with family, and even share amazing stories about our cultures with the whole world!

Why Was This Not Kind?

When the man put the videos of the ladies online without their permission, and then other people said mean things, it was not kind at all. Imagine if someone drew a funny picture of you and showed it to everyone, and then other kids laughed at you. How would you feel? Sad, maybe embarrassed, right? That's how these ladies felt. People should never, ever share pictures or videos of others without asking. It's their right to keep their private moments private. And it's even worse to say unkind words or make fun of someone, especially online. Saying mean things online can hurt people's feelings very deeply. It's called 'online abuse' or 'cyberbullying' for bigger kids, but for us, it just means being unkind and making people sad. We should always use our words to build people up, not to pull them down. Our African communities are strong because we support each other and show respect. We should always try to be gentle and thoughtful with our words, whether we are speaking or typing. Did you know? Being kind to others, both online and offline, makes your own heart feel happy too! It's a wonderful feeling to spread kindness.

How Can We Stay Safe?

So, how can we be super safe online? Here are some simple rules, just like rules for playing outside: 1. **Always ask a grown-up:** Before you go online, always ask your mum, dad, granny, or teacher. They are there to help you stay safe and choose good, safe things to do online. 2. **Tell a grown-up everything:** If you see something online that makes you feel scared, confused, or yucky, tell a grown-up right away! Don't keep it a secret. They can help you fix it and make you feel safe again. 3. **Don't talk to strangers:** Just like in real life, don't talk to people you don't know online. If someone you don't know tries to talk to you, tell your grown-up. 4. **Never share your picture or video:** And never share pictures or videos of your friends or family without asking a grown-up first. Grown-ups can help decide if it's safe to share something with other people. Remember, your face and your friends' faces are private! 5. **Think before you click:** Sometimes you see a button to press or a link. If you're not sure what it is or where it will take you, ask your grown-up. It's always better to be safe than sorry. Remember, your grown-ups are your superheroes of safety! They want to protect you, always, both in the real world and in the online world. Did you know? Your grown-ups are always learning too, so they can keep you safe in our changing world with new technologies!

Let's Be Kind and Respectful!

Being online is a wonderful thing, but it means we need to remember to be kind and respectful to everyone, everywhere. Every person deserves to be treated with respect and dignity. This means we don't take pictures or videos of anyone without their permission. We don't say mean things about anyone, whether we are talking face-to-face or typing words online. We always think about how our actions, or our words, might make someone else feel. Let's make our corner of the internet, and our whole world, a happier, kinder, and safer place for everyone, especially for all the amazing children of Africa! Did you know? When you are kind to someone, it often makes them want to be kind to someone else too! It's like a kindness superpower that spreads from person to person! So, my little African stars, remember these important lessons: * Always ask for permission before you take a picture or video of someone. * Always ask your grown-up before you go online. * Always tell a grown-up if something makes you feel unsafe or sad online. * And most importantly, always be kind! You are the future of Africa, and you can help make our world a safer and happier place. Keep shining bright! See you next time for more news from our amazing continent!
summary: A man secretly filmed ladies in Kenya and Ghana and put the videos online, which was not kind and made them sad, so this story teaches us to always ask permission and stay safe on the internet.
whyMatters: This is important because everyone deserves privacy and respect, and we all need to know how to be safe and kind when using the internet.

title: South Africa's exclusion from G7 summit no surprise, says Ramaphosa
content: Reports say the US pressured France to rescind Ramaphosa’s invitation after Trump threatened to boycott the summit.

originalTitle: Rationing power and diluting petrol - how African countries are coping with effects of Iran war
title: Africa's Big Energy Puzzle!
content:
Hello, Super Scientists!

Hello, my amazing friends all across our wonderful continent of Africa! It's your friendly news reporter, Auntie Joy, here to share a very important story with you today. Sometimes, grown-ups talk about big words like 'oil' and 'electricity' and things happening far away. But don't worry, Auntie Joy is here to make it super easy to understand, just for you! We're going to talk about a little puzzle that some of our countries are trying to solve.

What is This "Oil" We're Talking About?

First, let's talk about something super important called 'oil'. It's not the cooking oil your mum uses, though they are cousins! This 'oil' is a thick, dark liquid that comes from deep, deep underground. It’s like a hidden treasure! Did you know? Oil was made from tiny plants and animals that lived millions and millions of years ago! They got buried deep down, and over a very long time, they turned into oil. Why is oil so important? Well, it helps power many things we use every single day. We turn it into petrol for our cars, buses, and motorbikes. It also helps make electricity in some places, which gives us lights, charges our phones, and keeps our fridges cold. Think about your school bus. It needs fuel made from oil to take you to school. Think about the streetlights at night. Many of them rely on electricity, which sometimes comes from power plants that use oil or other fuels that are like oil.

A Little Problem Far, Far Away

Now, imagine there's a big playground far, far away from Africa, in a country called Iran. Sometimes, things get a bit difficult in that playground, like a big fight or a big misunderstanding. When these things happen, it can make it harder for the people who dig up oil to send it out to other countries, including some in Africa. It's a bit like if your favourite toy store suddenly couldn't get your favourite toys because the factory that makes them had a problem. You wouldn't get your toys right away, would you? That's what's happening with oil. Because there's a problem far away, it means less oil is coming to some of our African countries. And when there's less of something important, it can cause a few challenges right here at home. But don't worry, people are working hard to solve it!

How Does This Affect Our Homes? (Electricity!)

One big way this affects us is with electricity. You know how much we love our lights, watching TV, and playing games on our tablets, right? Well, some power stations in Africa use fuel made from oil to make electricity. When there's less oil, it means these power stations might not have enough fuel to make all the electricity we need all the time. So, what happens then? Sometimes, the lights go off! Grown-ups call this 'load shedding' or 'power cuts'. It's not fun when the lights go off, especially if you're doing your homework or if it gets dark early. Your parents might complain that the fridge stops working, or they can't charge their phones. But it happens so that everyone can have some electricity, just at different times. Did you know? Many countries in Africa are also trying to make electricity from the sun and wind! These are clean ways to make power that don't need oil.

What About Our Cars and Buses? (Fuel!)

Another way this problem affects us is with the fuel for cars and buses. As we talked about, oil is made into petrol. When it's hard to get enough oil, there's less petrol for everyone. In some places, grown-ups have to try and make the little petrol they have last longer. Sometimes, they might mix it with other things. This is called 'diluting' the petrol. It means the petrol isn't as strong or pure as it normally is. When petrol is diluted, cars and buses might not run as well or might use more fuel. It's like if you mix too much water in your juice – it doesn't taste as strong, right? The same can happen with fuel. This can make it a bit more expensive for your parents to fill up their car, or it might mean bus fares go up a little bit. But our countries are doing their best to make sure everyone can still travel and get around. Did you know? A single bus can carry many, many people, helping to save fuel compared to everyone driving their own car!

How Are Our Countries Helping?

Our countries, led by clever grown-ups like presidents and engineers, are working very hard to solve these problems. They are like super detectives, looking for solutions! First, they are trying to find oil from other friendly countries that don't have problems. They are talking to different people to make sure we can get the oil we need. Second, they are encouraging everyone to save electricity. That's why sometimes we have load shedding – it helps save power for later. Third, they are investing in new ways to make electricity. Like using the big, strong sun that shines over Africa, or the wind that blows across our plains. These are called 'renewable energy' because they never run out! Did you know? Africa has some of the best sunshine in the world! That's perfect for making solar electricity!

What Can WE Do? (Little Helpers Make a Big Difference!)

Even though you are small, you can be a super helper too! Every little bit helps our countries save energy. When you leave a room, make sure to turn off the light switch. It's like saying 'goodnight' to the light! Turn off the TV when no one is watching it. Maybe read a book or play outside instead – that saves electricity and is good for you! Don't leave chargers plugged into the wall if your phone or tablet is already full. They still use a tiny bit of power. When you brush your teeth, turn off the water tap. Water also needs energy to be pumped to your house! Talk to your family about saving energy. You can be the energy-saving champion of your house!

A Bright Future Ahead!

It might sound like a tricky puzzle, but remember, our countries are full of smart people working together. They are finding new ways to make sure we have enough electricity and fuel for everything we need. Africa is a continent of amazing ideas and strong people. We will keep learning and growing, and we will find clever ways to keep our lights on and our cars moving. Thank you for listening, my wonderful friends! Remember to be kind, be curious, and always be an energy-saving superhero! See you next time for more exciting news from Africa!
summary: A fight in a country far away means less oil for some African countries, causing problems like fewer lights and mixed fuel, but our leaders are working hard to find solutions and we can all help save energy.
whyMatters: This matters because oil helps power our lights, cars, and keeps our food fresh, so when it's hard to get, it affects our daily lives, but learning about it helps us understand and save energy.

originalTitle: Iran-backed hackers breach FBI director Kash Patel's personal emails
title: Super Secrets and Safe Surfing: Learning About Online Safety!
content:
Jambo, my amazing little friends from across Africa! It's your friendly news reporter, here to share a very important story with you today. We're going to talk about something that happened far away, but it teaches us all a big lesson about being super safe when we use computers and phones. Think of it like learning to look both ways before crossing the road – just for the internet!

Did you know? The internet is like a giant web connecting computers all over the world! It helps us learn, play games, and even talk to family far away!

Meet Mr. Kash!

Once upon a time, in a country far, far away called America, there is a very important man named Mr. Kash Patel. He's like a super-smart police chief, but not for catching robbers in the streets. Mr. Kash is in charge of helping to keep all the computers and the internet safe for his country. Imagine he's like the head captain of the online safety team! He works very hard to make sure bad people don't cause trouble on computers.

Mr. Kash's job is to protect important computer secrets and make sure everyone can use the internet safely. It's a very big and important job, just like a superhero protecting a city!

What Happened? A Little Computer Peek-a-boo!

Now, here's where our story gets interesting. Sometimes, there are people who like to be naughty online. They try to get into other people's computers without asking. This is like someone trying to open your toy box without your permission, or trying to read your secret diary. We call these naughty people 'hackers'.

These hackers, who were from another place and like to cause trouble, tried to peek into Mr. Kash's personal computer messages. These messages are called 'emails'. An email is like a super-fast letter you send using a computer. Many grown-ups use emails for work and to talk to friends and family. Personal emails are like your private letters – they have your own special information.

Did you know? The very first email was sent a long, long time ago, even before your parents were born!

So, these hackers managed to get into Mr. Kash's personal emails. And guess what they found? They found some old information, like his pretend 'school report card' from when he was looking for a job (we call this a resume) and some old pictures. Imagine someone finding your baby pictures and your old school drawings!

The good news is that the people who work with Mr. Kash said that this information was 'historical in nature'. That's a grown-up way of saying it was old stuff. It wasn't new, super-secret information that could cause big problems now. Phew! It's like finding a toy you played with a long time ago, not a brand-new secret toy.

Who Are These "Peekers"?

These 'peekers' or hackers are people who try to cause trouble online. They might want to steal information, or sometimes they just want to be mischievous. They don't ask for permission, and that's not kind or safe. It's important for us to know that while there are many wonderful things online, there are also a few people who don't play fair.

The grown-ups who keep computers safe worked very hard to understand what happened and to make sure Mr. Kash's computers were even safer. It's like when you accidentally leave your door open, and your parents help you remember to close and lock it next time. They learn from what happened and make things better.

Why This Story is Important for YOU!

Now, you might be thinking, 'Why is this important for me, a little super learner in Africa?' Well, my dear friends, it's super important because YOU use computers and phones too! You play games, watch videos, and maybe even do schoolwork online. The internet is a magical place, but just like walking in a big, busy market, you need to be careful and smart.

This story about Mr. Kash teaches us all a very big lesson about 'online safety'. Online safety means keeping yourself and your private information safe when you are using the internet. It's like wearing a helmet when you ride your bicycle – it keeps you safe!

Did you know? Many, many children in Africa are now learning to use computers and the internet for school and fun!

Keep Your Digital Secrets Safe!

So, how can YOU be an online safety superhero? Here are some simple, super important rules:

1. Passwords are like Secret Keys! When you log into a game or a website, you use a 'password'. A password is like a secret word that only YOU know. Make your passwords strong! Don't use your name or your birthday. Ask a grown-up to help you make a strong password, like a mix of big letters, small letters, and numbers. And NEVER, ever share your password with anyone, not even your best friend! It's your secret key!

2. Always Ask a Grown-Up! Before you click on something new, or if a strange message pops up, or if someone you don't know tries to talk to you online, ALWAYS ask a grown-up. Your parents, guardians, or teachers are there to help you stay safe. They are your online safety guardians!

3. Don't Share Your Secrets! Never tell strangers online your real name, where you live, your school, or your phone number. Your private information is like your super-secret identity – keep it safe! And don't share pictures of yourself or your family with people you don't know in real life.

4. Think Before You Click! If something looks strange or too good to be true, it probably is. Don't click on links from people you don't know. Always think: 'Is this safe? Should I ask a grown-up?'

Did you know? Practicing online safety is like having a superpower that protects your personal information!

Let's Be Super Online Explorers!

The internet is a wonderful place filled with amazing things to learn and explore. You can learn about animals, visit far-off lands, and even do fun puzzles! By remembering these simple safety rules, you can be a super online explorer, just like a brave adventurer going on a quest!

So, let's all be smart and safe when we use computers and phones. Keep your secrets safe, always ask a grown-up, and remember that playing fair and being kind online is the best way to be. Until next time, my amazing African friends, stay safe and keep shining bright!
summary: An important computer safety chief named Mr. Kash in America had his old private computer messages looked at by hackers, teaching us all about being super careful and safe when we use computers and the internet.
whyMatters: This story is important because it shows us that even important grown-ups need to be careful online, and it teaches us all, especially kids, how to keep our own information safe with strong passwords and by always asking a grown-up for help.

content: Is former Aston Villa and Chelsea midfielder Carney Chukwuemeka, who has made 97 senior appearances across all competitions without playing a full 90 minutes, an exception or becoming the norm?
